stations.label: THE FRANC IS BORN
stations.line: The Federal Coinage Act gave me a single body at last — one franc for a country that had spoken in a hundred coins, and I decided quietly that I would be the currency no one had to worry about.
stations.era_photo: A daguerreotype in a Bern mint courtyard, 1850: a woman in a dark high-collared day dress standing beside stacks of freshly struck silver coins, the long exposure blurring the workmen behind her, the plate silvered and faintly mirror-bright at the edges.

stations.label: THE LATIN MONETARY UNION
stations.line: I tied myself to France, Belgium and Italy by weight of silver — not from affection but from arithmetic, because a franc that can be checked against a gram is a franc that keeps its word.
stations.era_photo: An albumen print, 1865: the same woman seated at a treaty table among frock-coated delegates in Paris, gaslit interior, sepia tones, her hands resting on a ledger of coin standards, everything sharp except a draped curtain caught mid-motion.

stations.label: THE YEAR I FINALLY BENT
stations.line: I had held my gold value while the whole world abandoned theirs, and in September I devalued at last — thirty percent — the only time I let go of the rope, and I never forgot how the floor felt when it moved.
stations.era_photo: A silver gelatin press photograph, 1936: a woman in a tailored grey wool suit and cloche descendant hat outside the Swiss National Bank in Zürich, grain heavy in the shadows, headlines about devaluation pinned to a newsstand behind her.

stations.label: THE VAULT WHILE THEY BURNED
stations.line: When Europe finished burning I was still standing, convertible and calm, and everyone who had once mocked my caution came to me asking where they might put what was left — I said very little, and I opened the vault.
stations.era_photo: A black-and-white silver gelatin portrait, 1945: a composed woman in a dark postwar suit inside a bank vault in Basel, steel deposit doors behind her, hard directional lighting, fine grain, her expression unreadable.

stations.label: THE ANCHOR CUT LOOSE
stations.line: When Nixon closed the gold window the dollar let go of its promise, and I found myself floating free — a currency without a master to peg to, which frightened others and, I confess, suited me perfectly.
stations.era_photo: A Kodachrome slide, 1971: a woman in a wide-collared belted dress standing before a wall of exchange-rate boards in Geneva, saturated reds and golds, the slightly warm colour cast of the film, a rotary telephone on the desk beside her.

stations.label: TOO STRONG TO BEAR
stations.line: In the euro crisis the whole world fled into me at once, and my strength became a burden — so in September the Bank drew a line at one-twenty against the euro, a cap on my own value, which is a strange humiliation for one whose only pride was being trusted.
stations.era_photo: A digital DSLR photograph, 2011: a woman in a slim charcoal blazer at a glass-walled press conference in Zürich, flat modern lighting, blue trading screens glowing behind her, crisp high-resolution detail, microphones bristling in the foreground.

stations.label: THE DAY I BROKE MY WORD
stations.line: On the fifteenth of January I removed the cap without warning and markets shattered in minutes — I had promised a floor and then withdrew it, and I have spent every day since living with the fact that even I, the reliable one, can betray a promise.
stations.era_photo: A digital news photograph, January 2015: a woman in a dark coat outside the SNB building in winter, breath visible in cold air, a phone screen in her hand showing a vertical currency spike, sharp muted daylight, snow at the pavement's edge.

stations.label: STILL THE QUIET VAULT
stations.line: Today they still run to me whenever the world catches fire, and I am still here — negative rates behind me, my word carefully rebuilt — the currency people reach for precisely because I say so little and keep so much.
stations.era_photo: A high-resolution digital portrait, 2024: an ageless woman in a precise modern grey suit standing in a minimalist bank atrium in Zürich, natural window light, ultra-sharp detail, a faint reflection of the Alps in the glass behind her.
